"And when the people saw that Moses delayed to come down out of the mount, the people gathered themselves together unto Aaron, and said unto him, Up, make us gods, which shall go before us; for as for this Moses, the man that brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him." Henry and Gill note that "when the people saw that Moses delayed... the people gathered themselves together unto Aaron, and said... 4:

The witnesses note the making of the golden calf — the crux of the great idolatry. Calvin and Gill note that "he received them at their hand, and fashioned it with a graving tool, after he had made it a molten calf" — Aaron melted the gold and fashioned a molten calf (an image, probably after the Egyptian Apis or Mnevis, the bull-gods of Egypt); the molten calf (the idol, made of the gold); the Egyptian influence (the people turning to the calf-worship of Egypt, from which they had come).

On "These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt," interpreters expound the idolatry. The reformed and the dominant reading note the great sin — "These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt": the people ascribed to the calf the deliverance from Egypt (the glory of God given to an idol); the witnesses note the lesson — the great sin of idolatry (the breach of the first and second commandments, Exodus 20:3-5, lately given), the people not perhaps meaning to forsake the LORD wholly, but to worship him under a visible image (the calf as a representation of God, the will-worship, the corrupting of God's worship with images); the glory of God given to an idol (the gross sin, "they changed their glory into the similitude of an ox that eateth grass," Psalm 106:19-20; "they made a calf in Horeb, and worshipped the molten image"); the New Testament citing this as the great example of idolatry (Acts 7:40-41, Stephen; 1 Corinthians 10:7, "neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them; as it is written, The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play"); the apostasy of the heart, the turning from the unseen God to a visible idol.

The LORD plaguing the people for the calf displays the visitation of the sin: the LORD plagued the people for the calf (vv.34) — the judgment on the idolatry, the sin bearing its fruit, the warning against apostasy. The verse closes the chapter, before the renewal of the covenant (chs.33-34).
